---
title: "Foundations"
---

This opening part establishes the theory that supports every later method. I begin
with the relationship between a biological question, the data collected to answer
it, and the statistical structure used to interpret those data. That means learning
how to describe variation, how to visualise structure, and how to think about
sampling uncertainty before any formal test is attempted.

The purpose is not to linger in preliminaries, but to make the later inferential
and modelling chapters easier to read correctly. If the early descriptive work is
weak, later inference becomes mechanical and unreliable. If the foundations are
clear, the later methods become extensions of the same statistical reasoning rather
than a list of unrelated procedures.

By the end of this part, you should be able to recognise what kind of data you
have, describe their main features responsibly, and connect those features to the
language of variability, sampling, and uncertainty.
